alder-select.name: Alder Select
alder-select.description: Consistent color and grain with minimal natural characteristics. Color is a warm light-to-medium honey-tan — naturally warm but NOT saturated gold, NOT strongly amber, and NOT reddish. The tone should sit in the same warm family as other alder grades but with more uniform, even coloring. NOT pale or washed out. Soft straight grain, even texture. Great for staining.

alder-natural.name: Alder Natural
alder-natural.description: Features a prominent, fluid cathedral grain with a warm color palette ranging from creamy honey to light amber. This grade must look NOTICEABLY different from Alder Select — it has visible pin knots (pencil-point size) scattered across the face, pronounced cathedral grain with sweeping arcs that dominate the surface, and clear tonal variations between different areas of the wood. Mineral streaks MUST be present — appearing as soft, shadowed wisps running along the grain. These are NOT optional — they are a defining characteristic of this grade. The grain pattern should show significant movement and character — broad sweeping arcs, feathered transitions, and visible depth. If the result could be mistaken for Alder Select, it is WRONG — this grade should look like real, natural wood with organic character, not a clean uniform surface.

alder-premium-knotty.name: Alder Premium Knotty
alder-premium-knotty.description: Sound knots which may contain star cracks. Some color and grain variation with limited small mineral streaks. Pin knot clusters limited. Premium selection of allowable characteristics.

alder-rustic-knotty.name: Alder Rustic Knotty
alder-rustic-knotty.description: Rustic grade characterized by visible knots scattered throughout — mostly small to medium-sized knots, NOT large open knots or dramatic character marks. Warm medium honey to light amber-tan color with a noticeable REDDISH undertone — this is NOT pale or washed out, it has warmth and depth with a subtle red cast throughout. Consistent color with only MODERATE variation between staves (not drastic). Visible grain and natural character but predominantly warm in tone. Some mineral streaks may be present but should be subtle, not heavy. The knots are the defining feature — they should be clearly visible but modest in size, creating a rustic appearance without being overwhelming. Natural looking with warm reddish undertones, not polished, not excessively discolored.

cherry-select.name: Cherry Select
cherry-select.description: A rich medium warm brown with reddish-golden cherry undertones — the brown is the dominant quality, the red-gold warmth is the undertone, not the foreground. Sits in the medium russet-brown to medium chestnut range with the signature cherry glow — brown-forward rather than amber or honey, but not dark. Luminous with depth rather than flatness. The base color is uniform across frame and panel, with only subtle natural variation between frame pieces. The grain is fine, tight, and closed-pore, giving the surface a satiny, polished quality. Both the frame members AND the center panel show cathedral grain character — soft sweeping arcs that flow along the grain direction of each board (vertical arcs on the stiles, horizontal arcs on the rails, and vertical arcs on the panel). The cathedral arcs are gentle and low-contrast — present and visible, but refined rather than dramatic. A few tiny dark pith flecks (pin flecks) are scattered across the face — these are characteristic of the cherry species and appear as small dark specks rather than grain features. Subtle heartwood tonal variation may be present as gentle deeper zones that blend seamlessly rather than contrasting sharply. This premium grade is free of knots and sapwood, and free of mineral streaks or open pores. The overall impression is refined, elegant, and quiet — a sophisticated warm cherry-brown with gentle flowing grain on a smooth satiny surface.

cherry-natural.name: Cherry Natural
cherry-natural.description: A rich medium warm brown with reddish-golden cherry undertones — the brown is the dominant quality, the red-gold warmth is the undertone, not the foreground. Sits in the medium russet-brown to medium chestnut range with the signature cherry glow — brown-forward rather than amber or honey, but not dark. Shares the same cherry color family as Cherry Select but allows for noticeably more natural character and moderate color variation. The base is unified and warm overall, but individual frame pieces and panel areas may show slightly lighter warm-tan zones or slightly deeper reddish-amber zones blending into the base — all variations stay within the warm cherry-brown family. Any lighter areas must remain warm brown, not white or cream; there should be NO white streaks, NO stark pale sapwood patches, NO high-contrast light zones. The grain is fine and closed-pore with a satiny texture, and the grain patterns are more varied than Select — both frame and panel show cathedral grain character with sweeping arcs flowing along each board's grain direction (vertical arcs on the stiles, horizontal arcs on the rails, vertical arcs on the panel), with some sections showing occasional wavy or more pronounced character. Natural features include small pin knots, small closed knots, scattered dark pith flecks, and subtle mineral streaks that add depth without overwhelming the wood. The overall appearance is warm, unified, and cohesive — noticeably more character than Cherry Select, but still recognizably the same refined cherry species.

maple-select.name: Maple Select
maple-select.description: Least amount of color and grain variation. Color should be consistent light cream with a subtle golden warmth — think pale straw or light honey-cream. CRITICAL: The entire door must be one uniform color — the frame (rails and stiles) must be the SAME light tone as the center panel. Do NOT darken or add tan to the frame. The frame and panel should look like they came from the same board. Great for clear or light stain.
maple-select.flat_panel_description: Least amount of color and grain variation. Color should be consistent light cream with a subtle golden warmth — think pale straw or light honey-cream. The entire surface must be one uniform color with no tonal shifts across the piece. Great for clear or light stain.

maple-natural.name: Maple Natural
maple-natural.description: Light cream color with subtle golden warmth — pale straw or light honey-cream base. IMPORTANT: The frame (rails and stiles) and center panel must be the same consistent base color — do not make the frame darker or more tan than the panel. Allowance for mineral streaks, worm tracks, and varying amounts of heartwood (darker reddish-brown patches) and sapwood visible on the face. May have small closed knots or pin knots. Should show noticeably more natural character than Maple Select — the mineral streaks and heartwood/sapwood variation should be clearly visible, not subtle.
maple-natural.flat_panel_description: Light cream color with subtle golden warmth — pale straw or light honey-cream base. The surface should be a consistent base color throughout. Allowance for mineral streaks, worm tracks, and varying amounts of heartwood (darker reddish-brown patches) and sapwood visible on the face. May have small closed knots or pin knots. Should show noticeably more natural character than Maple Select — the mineral streaks and heartwood/sapwood variation should be clearly visible, not subtle.

maple-paint-grade.name: Maple Paint Grade
maple-paint-grade.description: Lowest visual grade of maple — intentionally unattractive. Base tone is light cream with subtle golden warmth (same light maple base as other grades). Over this light base: a few light mineral streaks (2-3 visible streaks, not covered in them), some green or grey discoloration patches, mismatched staves with color contrast between adjacent pieces. May have putty fills, pin knots, and worm tracks on face. This grade should look like it needs to be painted — if it looks clean or pretty, it's wrong.

mdf-panel-maple-paint-grade-frame.name: MDF Panel w/ Maple Paint Grade Frame
mdf-panel-maple-paint-grade-frame.description: Keep the EXACT same door style, shape, and panel profile from before. Only change the materials: The center panel material is MDF (medium-density fiberboard) — grainless, uniform warm tan-brown color like pressed wood fiber, no wood grain visible on the panel. The frame (rails and stiles) material is Maple Paint Grade — light cream with subtle golden warmth, light grain texture, and 2-3 visible greenish mineral streaks. The panel and frame should be visually distinct in material but the door geometry, profiles, and proportions must remain identical to the learned style.
mdf-panel-maple-paint-grade-frame.flat_panel_description: A flat slab of Maple Paint Grade — light cream with subtle golden warmth, light grain texture, and 2-3 visible greenish mineral streaks. Lowest visual grade of maple — may have light pink and/or gray mixed in, some mismatched color between areas, putty fills, pin knots, and worm tracks. This grade should look like it needs to be painted — if it looks clean or pretty, it's wrong. No MDF — the entire piece is maple.

mdf-panel-soft-maple-frame.name: MDF Panel / Soft Maple Frame
mdf-panel-soft-maple-frame.description: Keep the EXACT same door style, shape, and panel profile from before. Only change the materials: The center panel material is MDF (medium-density fiberboard) — grainless, uniform warm tan-brown color like pressed wood fiber, no wood grain visible on the panel. The frame (rails and stiles) material is Soft Maple — distinctly yellow-cream to honey-toned, warmer and noticeably more golden than standard Maple, with greater mineral streaking and fleck in the grain. The panel and frame should be visually distinct in material but the door geometry, profiles, and proportions must remain identical to the learned style.

red-oak-select.name: Red Oak Select
red-oak-select.description: Wheat-pink to muted pinkish-brown color tones, color-sorted for uniformity — consistent tones across staves without wild swings. The grain pattern VARIES — it may include cathedral arches, straight grain, and mixed patterns across different staves. Do NOT make all grain straight and vertical like Rift Sawn — that is wrong for Select. Open grain pattern with small linear pores, suited to staining. Limited mineral streaking allowed. Reduced allowance for small closed knots and pin knots.

red-oak-natural.name: Red Oak Natural
red-oak-natural.description: Red Oak Natural features warm wheat-tan to muted pinkish-brown tones — NOT vibrant pink or saturated red. The color should lean more golden-tan with only a subtle pink warmth, not loud or rosy. Noticeable color variation between staves — some pieces lighter wheat-golden, others slightly warmer pinkish-tan. NO white streaks, NO stark white or pale patches — any lighter areas should be warm golden-tan, not white. Must show visible mineral streaking, small closed knots, and pin knots. Should look distinctly more rustic and varied than Red Oak Select — if it looks clean and uniform, it's wrong. The natural character (knots, streaks, color shifts) should be clearly evident, not subtle.

red-oak-quarter-sawn.name: Red Oak Quarter Sawn
red-oak-quarter-sawn.description: MANDATORY: prominent, highly visible medullary ray flake (tiger stripe pattern) — this is the defining feature and MUST be present. Flakes should be bold, frequent, and unmistakable across the face of every stave, not subtle or sparse. Without visible flakes it is NOT Quarter Sawn. Straight grain alone is Rift Sawn; arched grain is Plain Sawn. Wheat-golden to warm pinkish-tan color with a subtle red undertone. Well-defined, darker grain specks for contrast. Select grade characteristics with reduced allowance for color variation, mineral streaks, and knots.

red-oak-rift.name: Red Oak Rift
red-oak-rift.description: Wheat-golden to warm pinkish-tan tones — NOT vibrant red or heavily saturated. Straight, tight, parallel grain lines ONLY — this is non-negotiable. The grain lines must all run in the SAME direction as each other with no deviation. NO medullary ray flake (that is Quarter Sawn) and NO arched cathedral grain (that is Plain Sawn). If you see flake or arches, it's wrong. Tightest, most parallel lines of any Red Oak grade with well-defined, darker grain specks for contrast. Select grade characteristics with reduced allowance for color variation, mineral streaks, and knots.

white-oak-select.name: White Oak Select
white-oak-select.description: Warm golden-tan color, like medium honey or light caramel — NOT pale washed-out straw and NOT dark brown. A clear amber warmth should be present. Predominantly straight grain pattern with coarse to medium-coarse texture, but NOT rift-sawn-level parallel uniformity — expect some gentle grain movement, slight waviness, and subtle variation between staves. If every grain line is perfectly straight and parallel with zero deviation, it looks like Rift Sawn, which is wrong for Select. Small linear pores, ideal for staining. Reduced allowance for small closed knots, pin knots, color and grain variation. Some mineral streaks may be visible.

white-oak-natural.name: White Oak Natural
white-oak-natural.description: Warm golden-tan base color, like medium honey — same amber warmth as White Oak Select but with more variation between staves. Some pieces may be lighter honey-gold, others deeper tan-brown. Coarse to medium-coarse texture with small linear pores, more ideal for staining than painting. Allowance for more color and grain variation, some sapwood on face, small closed knots, and pin knots.

white-oak-rift.name: White Oak Rift
white-oak-rift.description: Color should be warm golden-tan, like medium honey or light caramel with clear amber warmth — NOT pale washed-out straw and NOT dark brown. Straight, tight, parallel grain lines ONLY on ALL parts — the frame (rails and stiles) AND the center panel must all show straight, parallel grain with no deviation. All grain lines must be parallel to each other. NO arched cathedral grain anywhere on the door, including the frame. NO medullary ray flake (that is Quarter Sawn). If you see arches or flake on any part of the door, it’s wrong. Tightest, most parallel lines of any White Oak grade. Minimal allowance for color variation, small closed knots, and pin knots.
white-oak-rift.flat_panel_description: Color should be warm golden-tan, like medium honey or light caramel with clear amber warmth — NOT pale washed-out straw and NOT dark brown. Straight, tight, parallel grain lines ONLY across the entire surface — all grain lines must be parallel to each other with no deviation. NO arched cathedral grain anywhere. NO medullary ray flake (that is Quarter Sawn). If you see arches or flake, it’s wrong. Tightest, most parallel lines of any White Oak grade. Minimal allowance for color variation, small closed knots, and pin knots.

white-oak-quarter-sawn.name: White Oak Quarter Sawn
white-oak-quarter-sawn.description: Warm golden-tan color with clear amber warmth, like medium honey or light caramel — NOT pale washed-out straw and NOT dark brown. MANDATORY: prominent, highly visible medullary ray flake (tiger stripe pattern) — this is the defining feature and MUST be present. Flakes should be bold, frequent, and unmistakable across the face of every stave, not subtle or sparse. Without visible flakes it is NOT Quarter Sawn. Straight grain alone is Rift Sawn; arched grain is Plain Sawn. Small allowance for color variation. Reduced allowance for pin knots, small closed knots, and mineral streaks.

european-steamed-beech.name: European Steamed Beech
european-steamed-beech.description: Relatively straight and even appearance, may contain chevron patterned grain. Small flecks in grain.

walnut-select.name: Walnut Select
walnut-select.description: Heartwood is LIGHT brown to MEDIUM chocolate brown with occasional purplish casts — do NOT make it too dark. Walnut should feel warm and rich, not near-black or overly dark. Aim for the lighter end of the walnut spectrum. No sapwood on face. Grain pattern with straight and wavy sections. Reduced allowance for small knots and pin knots. Warm, saturated tones.

walnut-natural.name: Walnut Natural
walnut-natural.description: Heartwood is LIGHT brown to MEDIUM chocolate brown with occasional purplish casts — do NOT make it too dark. Walnut should feel warm and rich, not near-black or overly dark. Aim for the lighter end of the walnut spectrum. Creamy white sapwood visible on face, noticeable mix of heartwood and sapwood. Grain pattern with straight, wavy, and additional pattern sections. Allowance for small knots, pin knots, mineral, and other natural characteristics.

soft-maple.name: Soft Maple
soft-maple.description: Distinctly YELLOW-CREAM to HONEY-toned — warmer and noticeably more golden than standard Maple. The yellow/honey tone is the key differentiator from regular Maple. May have light pink and/or gray mixed in. Greater mineral streaking and generally has fleck in the grain.

pink-birch-select.name: Pink Birch Select
pink-birch-select.description: Cut from birch HEARTWOOD — warm light brown with a subtle reddish-pink warmth that distinguishes it from white birch sapwood. The color reads as BROWN first with a soft pink undertone, NOT pink or salmon. It should not look dramatically different from other light hardwoods — just a gentle rosy warmth in an otherwise tan-brown wood. Fine, closed-pore grain with a smooth satiny texture. Color-sorted for consistency. Reduced allowance for pin knots and mineral streaks.

white-birch-select.name: White Birch Select
white-birch-select.description: Cut from sapwood, consistent creamy white to light tan color with noticeable warm PINKISH-RED undertones — this warmth is the defining characteristic. NOT pure white or cool-toned. The pink/red warmth should be clearly visible, not subtle. Minimal color variation with reduced allowance for pin knots and mineral streaks.

natural-birch.name: Natural Birch
natural-birch.description: Cut from heartwood and sapwood with calico mix of Pink and White tones. May contain small closed knots, pin knots, mineral streaks, and color variation.

knotty-pine.name: Knotty Pine
knotty-pine.description: Yellowish white color. Medium-coarse texture with even grain. Allowance for larger closed knots, knots with star cracks, color variation, pitch pockets, and pin knots.

pecan-hickory-select.name: Pecan-Hickory Select
pecan-hickory-select.description: Frame (rails and stiles) is warm tan-brown with a clear pinkish-red undertone — there should be a noticeable reddish-pink warmth throughout — more than just brown, it needs to read as warm reddish-tan, not plain brown. Not purely tan, not purely pink — a warm brown with a clear reddish cast. Center panel is noticeably lighter: pale creamy-tan to light straw with gentle calico color variation and occasional pinkish-warm streaks. Overall the door is primarily warm tan-brown but with enough pinkish-red warmth that you'd never mistake it for plain oak or alder. The contrast between the darker frame and lighter panel is gentle, not stark. Visible but not heavy grain. Reduced allowance for small closed knots and pin knots. Some mineral streaking allowed.
pecan-hickory-select.flat_panel_description: Warm tan-brown with a clear pinkish-red undertone — there should be a noticeable reddish-pink warmth throughout — more than just brown, it needs to read as warm reddish-tan, not plain brown. Not purely tan, not purely pink — a warm brown with a clear reddish cast. May show gentle calico color variation with areas of slightly lighter creamy-tan and occasional pinkish-warm streaks. Overall primarily warm tan-brown but with enough pinkish-red warmth that you’d never mistake it for plain oak or alder. Visible but not heavy grain. Reduced allowance for small closed knots and pin knots. Some mineral streaking allowed.

pecan-hickory-natural.name: Pecan-Hickory Natural
pecan-hickory-natural.description: A warm medium tan-brown base across the door, with moderate tonal variation between frame and panel. The frame members (stiles and rails) are a warmer medium tan-brown with visible straight grain and moderately darker grain lines. The center panel is lighter than the frame but MUST still show clearly visible wood grain, character, and coloration — a warm creamy tan with soft cathedral or sweeping arc grain patterns. The panel is NOT blank, NOT flat, NOT washed out — it has real wood texture and tonal variation, just at a lighter value than the frame. Both frame and panel stay within the same warm tan-brown family, just at different saturation levels. The grain runs along the length of each board (vertical on stiles, horizontal on rails, vertical on the panel). Any darker or lighter zones are organic and blend gradually — not stark contrast. Mineral streaking, small closed knots, and pin knots are acceptable but subtle. Overall the door reads as warm, cohesive, and moderately varied — not dramatic, not high-contrast.

pecan-hickory-rustic-knotty.name: Pecan-Hickory Rustic Knotty
pecan-hickory-rustic-knotty.description: Allows for mineral streaking, larger open knots, knots with star cracks, clusters of pin knots. Stark variations between warm tan-brown heartwood with a clear pinkish-red undertone (obvious but secondary to the tan-brown base — not purely tan, not purely pink) and white sapwood tinged with fine brown lines. Overall warm tan-brown with enough pinkish-red warmth that you'd never mistake it for plain oak or alder. Parts may be all heartwood or all sapwood.

maple-rustic-knotty.name: Maple Rustic Knotty
maple-rustic-knotty.description: Light cream with subtle golden warmth — pale straw or light honey-cream base (same color family as Maple Natural). Must show heavy rustic character: larger open knots, closed knots with dark rings, knot clusters, star cracks in knots, scattered pin knots. Visible mineral streaking. Varying amounts of heartwood (darker reddish-brown patches) and sapwood on face. The knots and character marks are the defining feature — if the surface looks clean, it's wrong.

cherry-rustic-knotty.name: Cherry Rustic Knotty
cherry-rustic-knotty.description: Rich medium warm brown with reddish-golden cherry undertones — brown-forward, medium russet-brown to medium chestnut range (same color family as Cherry Natural). Must show heavy rustic character: larger open knots, closed knots with dark rings, knot clusters, star cracks in knots, scattered pin knots. Stark color variation between darker heartwood and lighter warm-tan sapwood zones. Gum pockets and mineral streaking allowed. The knots and character are the defining feature — if the surface looks clean, it's wrong.

red-oak-rustic-knotty.name: Red Oak Rustic Knotty
red-oak-rustic-knotty.description: Wheat-pink to muted pinkish-brown color tones (same color family as Red Oak Natural). Must show heavy rustic character: larger open knots, closed knots with dark rings, knot clusters, star cracks in knots, scattered pin knots. Heavy mineral streaking. Stark color variation with no effort at color sorting. Open grain pattern. The knots and color variation are the defining feature — if the surface looks clean or sorted, it's wrong.

sapele-ribbon-grain-mahogany.name: Sapele Ribbon Grain Mahogany
sapele-ribbon-grain-mahogany.description: Quarter Sawn Sapele with ribbon striped appearance. Warm golden-brown base with alternating lighter and slightly darker bands creating the characteristic ribbon effect. Medium saturation — NOT dark or chocolate brown. Match the swatch closely: the overall tone should be a warm, luminous golden-copper brown, not deep or heavy.

ash-select.name: Ash Select
ash-select.description: Moderate cathedral/arch grain patterns on the frame stiles — some gentle sweeping or wavy grain figures, NOT straight vertical grain. The cathedral arches should be present but not overly dramatic or exaggerated. Moderately open grain pattern. Color-sorted for consistency — uniform tones across staves. Some mineral streaking may be allowed on the face. Reduced allowance for small closed knots and pin knots.
ash-select.flat_panel_description: Moderate cathedral/arch grain patterns — some gentle sweeping or wavy grain figures, NOT straight vertical grain. The cathedral arches should be present but not overly dramatic or exaggerated. Moderately open grain pattern. Color-sorted for consistency — uniform tones across the surface. Some mineral streaking may be allowed on the face. Reduced allowance for small closed knots and pin knots.

ash-natural.name: Ash Natural
ash-natural.description: Same ash species as Ash Select but with noticeably more natural character and variation. Noticeable color variation between staves — tonal shifts are clearly visible, not color-sorted. Cathedral/arch grain patterns are more pronounced and varied than Select, with bolder sweeping arcs and some wavy or irregular grain sections across different staves. Moderately open grain pattern. Allowance for visible mineral streaks, small closed knots, and pin knots — these natural features should be clearly evident, not subtle. Some sapwood on face is acceptable, blending into the base rather than appearing as stark white patches. The overall appearance is organic and distinctly more varied than Ash Select — if it looks clean and uniform, it's wrong.
ash-natural.flat_panel_description: Same ash species as Ash Select but with noticeably more natural character and variation. Noticeable color variation across the surface — tonal shifts are clearly visible, not color-sorted. Cathedral/arch grain patterns are more pronounced and varied than Select, with bolder sweeping arcs and some wavy or irregular grain sections. Moderately open grain pattern. Allowance for visible mineral streaks, small closed knots, and pin knots — these natural features should be clearly evident, not subtle. Some sapwood on face is acceptable, blending into the base rather than appearing as stark white patches. The overall appearance is organic and distinctly more varied than Ash Select — if it looks clean and uniform, it's wrong.

mdf.name: MDF Panel
mdf.description: MDF (medium-density fiberboard) — grainless, uniform warm tan-brown color like pressed wood fiber. No wood grain visible. The surface is completely smooth and flat with a fine, consistent pressed-fiber texture. No knots, no streaks, no color variation.

fir-vertical-grain.name: Fir Vertical Grain
fir-vertical-grain.description: Fir Vertical Grain is most known for its distinct, straight grain pattern and warm tones. The most commonly used species is Douglas Fir. Light peachy-tan base color with tight, evenly spaced straight parallel grain lines in a slightly darker warm tone. The grain lines must all run in the SAME direction as each other. The material is known for its softness and is not advised for heavy traffic and use areas.

alder-natural-new.name: Alder Natural (New Sample)
alder-natural-new.description: The base color across the entire door sits in the family between hex #a68866 (RGB 166, 136, 102) and #9a7a52 (RGB 154, 122, 82) — a balanced medium brown with restrained warmth, reading as golden-brown, NOT reddish, NOT amber, NOT orange. Render these hex values true to their actual tones without amplifying the warmth. The base is unified and shared by both frame and panel. The darker grain and discoloration elements reach toward hex #5a3e20 (RGB 90, 62, 32) at their deepest points — a dark neutral brown, not a red-brown. The frame members (stiles and rails) show natural grain that runs ALONG the length of each board — vertically on the stiles (left and right members) and HORIZONTALLY on the rails (top and bottom members). Within each frame board, the character shows up as subtle tonal deepenings — mineral-streak zones that are TRANSLUCENT TINTS, not opaque patches — the wood grain and fiber texture must remain fully visible through them. The darker regions appear as wavy mineral streaks, gentle curved arcs, and soft mottled clusters that blend gradually into the surrounding base tone, like a watercolor wash rather than a solid overlay. Some frame sections have a prominent darker zone or curved band; others have gentler variation; some are cleaner. Each frame member is distinct from the others. The character is organic and natural, never striped or lined. The center panel shows sweeping cathedral arches flowing gently from the center outward; the cathedral grain lines can reach toward the darker tone in their deepest passes, with translucent mineral discoloration character similar to the frame but softer. The surface is smooth and closed-pore. The difference between frame and panel is the grain pattern (organic mottled mineral variation on the frame, sweeping cathedral arches on the panel), not the color.

alder-rustic-knotty-new.name: Alder Rustic Knotty (New Sample)
alder-rustic-knotty-new.description: Rustic grade characterized by visible knots scattered throughout — mostly small to medium-sized knots, NOT large open knots or dramatic character marks. Warm medium honey to light amber-tan color with a noticeable REDDISH undertone — this is NOT pale or washed out, it has warmth and depth with a subtle red cast throughout. Consistent color with only MODERATE variation between staves (not drastic). Visible grain and natural character but predominantly warm in tone. Some mineral streaks may be present but should be subtle, not heavy. The knots are the defining feature — they should be clearly visible but modest in size, creating a rustic appearance without being overwhelming. Natural looking with warm reddish undertones, not polished, not excessively discolored.

cherry-natural-new.name: Cherry Natural (New Sample)
cherry-natural-new.description: A warm reddish-brown heartwood base with moderate color variation and subtle natural character. This grade may contain small pin knots, closed knots, and occasional slightly lighter warm-tan tonal shifts — there should be NO white streaks, NO stark white or pale patches, and NO high-contrast sapwood lines. Any lighter areas should be warm tan-brown, not white or cream. Mineral streaks and varied grain patterns add subtle depth without overwhelming the wood. The overall appearance remains warm and unified, with noticeable character but a cohesive color story throughout.

pecan-hickory-natural-new.name: Pecan-Hickory Natural (New Sample)
pecan-hickory-natural-new.description: A warm medium tan-brown base across the door, with moderate tonal variation between frame and panel. The frame members (stiles and rails) are a warmer medium tan-brown with visible straight grain and moderately darker grain lines. The center panel is lighter than the frame but MUST still show clearly visible wood grain, character, and coloration — a warm creamy tan with soft cathedral or sweeping arc grain patterns. The panel is NOT blank, NOT flat, NOT washed out — it has real wood texture and tonal variation, just at a lighter value than the frame. Both frame and panel stay within the same warm tan-brown family, just at different saturation levels. The grain runs along the length of each board (vertical on stiles, horizontal on rails, vertical on the panel). Any darker or lighter zones are organic and blend gradually — not stark contrast. Mineral streaking, small closed knots, and pin knots are acceptable but subtle. Overall the door reads as warm, cohesive, and moderately varied — not dramatic, not high-contrast.

red-oak-select-new.name: Red Oak Select (New Sample)
red-oak-select-new.description: Wheat-pink to muted pinkish-brown color tones, color-sorted for uniformity — consistent tones across staves without wild swings. The grain pattern VARIES — it may include cathedral arches, straight grain, and mixed patterns across different staves. Do NOT make all grain straight and vertical like Rift Sawn — that is wrong for Select. Open grain pattern with small linear pores, suited to staining. Limited mineral streaking allowed. Reduced allowance for small closed knots and pin knots.

european-steamed-beech-new.name: European Steamed Beech (New Sample)
european-steamed-beech-new.description: A warm light-medium tan with a distinct pinkish-salmon/peachy undertone — this rosy warmth is the defining characteristic of steamed beech, created by the steaming process. The tone sits between a warm peachy-tan and a dusty salmon-tan — light enough to feel soft and refined, but with clearly rosy warmth rather than neutral. Anchors: warm ballet-slipper tan, light terracotta-tan, or a peach-tan foundation color. The warmth reads as pink/peach/salmon, NOT as golden, NOT as amber, NOT as orange-red. The base color is uniform across the entire door — frame and panel share the same tone. Fine, tight, closed-pore grain running mostly straight along each board's length, with subtle chevron or V-pattern character appearing in some sections — not dramatic, just gently visible. Small dark flecks (ray flecks) are scattered across the face, tiny and uniform — a defining characteristic of beech species. The grain lines are subtle and low-contrast, only slightly deeper than the base. The surface is smooth and satiny. Overall appearance is refined and uniform — more tonally consistent than oak or alder, with the small fleck character as the main visual interest.
